result:
The morning light hit the kitchen counter at 6:47, same angle as yesterday, same dust motes performing their slow cathedrals in the yellow beam. I've been thinking about inheritance lately—not money, but the stranger things. My grandmother's habit of saving butter wrappers for greasing pans. The way my father cleared his throat before saying anything important, as if the words needed a runway. I do it now too. Genetic stutter.

Last Tuesday I found a dead raccoon by the bike path, already being claimed by flies and the small negotiations of decay. I stood there longer than reasonable. Not morbid, exactly. More like: *this too is a Tuesday*. The world doesn't pause for your horror or your grocery list. Someone jogged past with earbuds, checking their pace. The raccoon didn't care. The flies were ecstatic.

I've been reading about deep time. How the Appalachians were once taller than the Himalayas, now just the eroded backs of ancient mountains, older than bones, than blood, than the very concept of tall. We think permanence when we see them, those soft ridges hazed with blue. They're actually a lesson in surrender. Everything gives. Everything yields to wind, to water, to the patient abrasion of simply continuing.

My neighbor practices saxophone at odd hours. Sometimes "Careless Whisper," sometimes scales that sound like questions. I've never met him. We share a wall, this stranger and I, his breath vibrating through plaster, my silence vibrating back. Is this intimacy? I don't know the word for what we are. Acoustic neighbors. Fellow travelers in the frequency spectrum.

The coffee's gone cold. I keep forgetting to drink it while it wants me.

Memory is such a fraud. I can tell you about the smell of my elementary school cafeteria—overcooked green beans, floor wax, the particular plastic of lunch trays—and yet I walked through that building hundreds of times and cannot reconstruct a single complete day. Just shards. The time I dropped my chocolate milk and everyone looked. The girl with the birthmark shaped like Florida. The janitor who hummed opera while mopping. Selective as any editor, my mind kept these, discarded the rest. What did I lose? Whole afternoons of multiplication tables, the walk home through leaves, the precise feeling of being nine and not yet knowing what failure meant in any permanent way.

I want to write about hope without being embarrassing. Is that possible? Hope feels so unfashionable, so naive in a world that rewards the knowing smirk, the preemptive dismissal. But I keep waking up. We all do, mostly. Keep making the coffee, keep answering emails, keep planning for futures that may not arrive in the forms we imagine. That's not nothing. That's not nothing at all.

My mother called last week to say she'd started painting again. Watercolors, loose and strange, nothing like her careful nature. "I'm not good," she said, "but I forget to eat lunch when I'm doing it." I cried after hanging up. Couldn't explain why to my partner. Still can't, fully. Something about permission. About the late-arriving discovery that your one life doesn't need to be a performance for judges who aren't even watching.

The raccoon was gone the next day. City workers, probably, or coyotes working the night shift. The grass was flattened where it had lain, slowly rising back toward vertical. In a week, no evidence. I check now when I pass, some stupid ritual. The spot is just grass, just October, just the ongoing project of the world moving through itself.

I read that trees in a forest share nutrients through fungal networks, the old feeding the young, the healthy sustaining the wounded. They call it the wood wide web, because scientists can't resist. But the fact of it: a birch sending carbon to a fir, no benefit to itself, just... *here, take this*. We don't have good language for cooperation. Our stories favor the lone wolf, the self-made, the singular genius wrestling truth from an indifferent universe. Maybe the universe isn't indifferent. Maybe it's networked, fungal, quietly passing sugars in the dark.

I'm running out of words. This is always the feeling—more to say, no more saying allowed. Like leaving a party still full of conversation, the good kind, the kind that makes you feel briefly understood. The saxophone starts next door. Not "Careless Whisper" this time. Something slower, unfinished, the player working the same phrase twice, three times, searching for the door into it.

I'll listen until he finds it, or until the light changes, or until the coffee, reheated, goes cold again. The mountain erodes. The fly finds its raccoon. We keep offering what we have, not knowing if anyone receives it, not knowing if receiving even matters, doing it anyway because the alternative is a loneliness too complete to survive.

1000.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
